content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

October 14, 1976

MR. Steven D. Burton
Attorney at Law
P.O. Box 428
Salinas, CA 93901

Re: Opinion Request
No. 76-045

Dear Mr. Burton:

In its October meeting, the Fair Political Practices
Commission instructed the staff to send you this letter in
lieu of the formal Opinion which you requested (No. 76-045).
This action is based on the fact that subsequent to the time
the Commission accepted your opinion request, the staff
acquired facts which indicate that the request does not
involve substantial questions of interpretation of the Act.

It is our understanding that the United Farm Workers
(hereinafter "UFW") has a political arm known as the Citizens
Participation Committee (hereinafter "CPC"); and it is the
CPC, not the UFW, that is directing and organizing the cam-
paign in support of Proposition 14. Since the activities of
the CPC are solely political, it is a primary purpose commit-
tee within the meaning of the Commission's regulations, 2 Cal.
Cal. Adm. Code Sections 18215(b)(4) and 18225(b)(4). Further-
more, the CPC will be required to file campaign statements
in connection with the campaign to pass Proposition 14 since
it undoubtedly will raise over $500 to support its activities
in connection with the campaign. In addition, if the UFW
itself receives contributions of $500 or more, makes expendi-
tures or in-kind contributions totaling $500 or more, or
makes cash contributions totaling $5,000 or more, it too
will be a committee with reporting obligations.

Specifically, you asked whether there are any
reporting obligations attendant to services performed by
attorneys on retainer to the UFW. From the information
provided to the staff, we understand that the attorneys are
not employees of the UFW and that their political activities
are limited to providing services to the CPC in the form of
assistance in filing campaign statements pursuant to the


Mr. Steven D. Burton
October 14, 1976
Page Two

Political Reform Act. Although paid for by the UFW, the
services are "received" by the CPC. Under these circum-
stances, the UFW is making an in-kind contribution to the
CPC within the meaning of 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18215(b)
and (c) which must be reported. Moreover, the exception
established by 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18423 relative to
compensation paid to employees is not applicable since the
attorneys are not employees of UFW but rather serve on retainer.

With respect to the "volunteer non-employee staff,"
again the exception in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18423 is not
applicable since, by definition, such persons are not employees.
Thus, if the volunteer benefits ($10 per week and some lodging)
are paid by the CPC, they are reportable by the CPO as "expendi-
tures." It will not be necessary, however, to itemize these
"expenditures" with respect to each individual volunteer
unless they amount to $50.00 or more for any particular
volunteer. It will be sufficient to report the total amount
spent in volunteer benefits.

If the volunteer benefits are paid by the UFW,
they are in-kind contributions from the UFW to the CPC, and
reportable as such, since the political activities of the
non-employee staff are performed under the direction of the
CPC. See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18215(c). Of course, if
the volunteer non-employee divides his time between political
and non-political activities, only a pro rata portion of the
volunteer benefits need be reported as expenditures or contribu-
tions. We understand, however, that most of the time the
non-employee staff will be devoted to political activities
during the pre-election period. Finally, any other payments
made to or on behalf of any other volunteers or campaign
workers should be reported in accordance with the standards
set forth herein.

We understand that you received interim advice
that your campaign statements filed in connection with the
effort to pass Proposition 14 should be completed in the
manner described in this letter. Under these circumstances,
we think it will be sufficient, for purposes of complying
with the Political Reform Act, if you continue filing pur-
suant to this advice; it will not be necessary to amend the
proponent's campaign statement filed in connection with the


MR. Steven D. Burton
October 14, 1976
Page Three

qualification drive. If you have any further questions,
please contact Ted Prim, an attorney in the Commission's
Legal Division.

Sincerely,

Michael Bennett
Executive Director

MB:TP:plh


UNITED FARM WORKERS of AMERICA Request No. 78 045
National Headquarters: La Paz, Keene, California Date Received 7/1-76
(805) 822-5571 Response Due 4/15/76

APR 1 0 1976

Jerome Cohen
Steven D. Burton
Attorneys At Law
P.O. Box 428
Salinas, CA 93901

March 29, 1976


Legal Counsel
Fair Political Practices Committee
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Agricultural Labor
Relations Initiative

Dear Counsel:

I am writing to you for an opinion so as to be able
to conform with the requirements of Proposition 9, as
they relate to the proponents of a statewide initiative.

My question relates specifically to services ren-
dered by individuals to qualify and pass the initiative.
These individuals would fall into three categories:

1) Attorneys on retainer to the United Farm Workers
of America who, as a regular part of their
duties, spend time working on the initiative.

2) Volunteer, non-employee staff of the United
Farm Workers who spend time working on the
initiative. Staff personnel are all volunteers,
receiving a volunteer benefit, money for food,
and usually lodging.

3) Individuals who volunteer only to collect sig-
natures, some of whom may receive expense money.

My understanding of Section 82015 of the Government
Code would eliminate the need to keep records as to the
second and third groups. As to the work of attorneys,
since this work falls within our normal responsibilities
to a client, it would also seem exempt from reporting
requirements.


Legal Counsel, Fair Political Practices Committee
March 29, 1976

p. 2

Please advise by formal opinion immediately.

Sincerely,

Steven D. Burton
Counsel to
Cesar Chavez
Proponent of
Agricultural Labor
Relations Initiative

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

September 24, 1976

Mr. Steven D. Burton
Attorney at Law
P. O. Box 428
Salinas, CA 93901

Re: Request for Opinion
No. 76-045

Dear Mr. Burton:

This letter will confirm the substance of our telephone
conversation of September 21, 1976, concerning the issues posed
by your opinion request (No. 76-045), and will serve as interim
advice pending action by the Commission on the opinion request.

Your opinion request asks how to report certain activities during both the qualification and the election phases of
Proposition 14. I informed you in our telephone conversation
that our interim advice in this matter will be prospective and
it, therefore, will not be necessary at this time to amend your
statements filed in connection with the qualification drive. Accordingly, this letter is limited to outlining standards for disclosure in the campaign statements to be filed in connection with
the general election in November.

It is our understanding that the United Farm Workers
(hereinafter "UFW") has a political arm known as the Citizens Participation Committee (hereinafter "CPC"); and it is the CPC, not
the UFW, that is directing and organizing the campaign in support
of Proposition 14. Since the activities of the CPC are solely
political, it is a primary purpose committee within the meaning
of the Commission's regulations, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Sections 18215(b)(4)
and 18225(b)(4). Furthermore, the CPC undoubtedly will be required
to file campaign statements in connection with the campaign to pass
Proposition 14. In addition, if the UFW itself receives contributions of $500 or more, makes expenditures or in-kind contributions
totaling $500 or more, or makes a cash contributions totaling $5,000
or more, it too will be a committee with reporting obligations.

Specifically, you have asked whether there are any reporting obligations attendant to services performed by attorneys

TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE/ADMINISTRATION (916) 322-5660 • EXECUTIVE/LEGAL 322-5901 • ENFORCEMENT/CONFLICT OF INTEREST 322-6441


Mr. Steven D. Burson
September 24, 1976
Page Two

on retainer to the UFW. From the information provided to the staff,
we understand that the attorneys are not employees of the UFW and
that their political activities are limited to providing services
to the CPC in the form of assistance in filing campaign statements
pursuant to the Political Reform Act. Although paid for by the
UFW, the services are "received" by the CPC. Under these circum-
stances, the UFW is making an in-kind contribution to the CPC with-
in the meaning of 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18215(b) and (e). More-
over, the ten percent exception established by 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18423 is not applicable since the attorneys are not employ-
ees of UFW but rather serve on retainer.

With respect to the "volunteer non-employee staff," again
the ten percent exception is not applicable since, by definition,
such persons are not employees. Thus, if the volunteer benefits
($10 per week and some lodging) are paid by the CPC, they are re-
ported by the CPC as expenditures. However, if they are paid
by the UFW, they are in-kind contributions from the UFW to the CPC
since the political activities of the non-employee staff are per-
formed under the direction of the CPC. See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Sec-
tion 18215(c). Of course, if the volunteer non-employee divides
his time between political and non-political activities, only a
pro rata portion of the volunteer benefits need be reported as ex-
penditures or contributions. We understand, however, that most of
the time of the non-employee staff will be devoted to political ac-
tivities during the pre-election period. Finally, any other pay-
ments made to or on behalf of any other volunteers or campaign
workers should be reported in accordance with the standards set
forth in this paragraph.

I want to emphasize that this letter contains only in-
terim advice which is not binding on the Commission with respect
to its ultimate decision in this matter. However, until the Com-
mission acts, you may rely on this advice and action taken pursu-
ant to it will preclude any enforcement proceeding by the Commission.
If you have any further questions, please do not hesitate to
contact me.

Sincerely,

[signature]
Ted Prim
Staff Counsel
Legal Division

UNITED FARM WORKERS of AMERICA
National Headquarters La Paz, Keene, California
(805) 822-5571

Jerome Cohen
Steven D. Burton
Attorneys At Law
P.O. Box 428
Salinas, CA 93901

March 29, 1976

Legal Counsel
Fair Political Practices Committee
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Agricultural Labor
Relations Initiative

Dear Counsel:

I am writing to you for an opinion so as to be able
to conform with the requirements of Proposition 9, as
they relate to the proponents of a statewide initiative.

My question relates specifically to services ren-
dered by individuals to qualify and pass the initiative.
These individuals would fall into three categories:

1) Attorneys on retainer to the United Farm Workers
of America who, as a regular part of their
duties, spend time working on the initiative.

2) Volunteer, non-employee staff of the United
Farm Workers who spend time working on the
initiative. Staff personnel are all volunteers,
receiving a volunteer benefit, money for food,
and usually lodging.

3) Individuals who volunteer only to collect sig-
natures, some of whom may receive expense money.

My understanding of Section 82015 of the Government
Code would eliminate the need to keep records as to the
second and third groups. As to the work of attorneys,
since this work falls within our normal responsibilities
to a client, it would also seem exempt from reporting
requirements.


Legal Counsel, Fair Political Practices Committee          p. 2
March 29, 1976


Please advise by formal opinion immediately.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]

                                        Steven D. Burton
                                        Counsel to
                                        Cesar Chavez
                                        Proponent of
                                        Agricultural Labor
                                        Relations Initiative
